s1_b1: Two rules govern every engine, every fridge, every living thing. They sound almost too simple.
s1_b2: First rule. Energy is never created and never destroyed. It only changes form, or moves.
s1_b3: Heat into an engine becomes work out, plus heat dumped away. Add it all back: nothing missing. Ever.
s1_b4: So the first rule says you can't win — you can't get more energy out than you put in.
s1_b5: Then the second rule, the strange one. It's about direction.
s1_b6: Drop ink in water; it spreads. You will never, ever watch it gather itself back into a drop.
s1_b7: A hot coffee always cools to the room. The room never spontaneously reheats the coffee.
s1_b8: Nothing in the first rule forbids the reverse. Energy would still balance. Yet it never happens.
s1_b9: Because the universe drifts, always, toward more spread-out, more disordered, more mixed. We measure that drift with entropy.
s1_b10: The second rule: total entropy always increases. That's why time has a direction.
s1_b11: It's why no engine is perfect. Some energy must always be paid out as disordered waste heat. You can't even break even.
s1_b12: Computing an engine's maximum possible efficiency from its hot and cold operating temperatures — that's yours.
